'''Apollo Perelini''' (born 16 July 1969) is a former [[List of dual-code rugby internationals|dual-code international]] [[rugby union]] and [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s, and has [[Coach (sports)|coach]]ed in the 2000s and 2010s.

He played representative rugby union (RU) for [[New Zealand Colts|New Zealand Under-21s]], [[Samoa national rugby union team|Samoa]] and [[World XV]] and at club level for [[Auckland Rugby Union|Auckland]], [[North Harbour Rugby Union|North Harbour]] and [[Sale Sharks]], and representative rugby league (RL) for [[Samoa national rugby league team|Western Samoa]] and at club level for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]], as a {{rlp|pr}} or {{rlp|sr}}.

==Background==
Perelini was born in [[Auckland]], New Zealand. He was named '[[Apollo 11]]' because he was born on the day that the Apollo space project was launched.

He is a New Zealand national.

==Playing career==
Perelini played for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] in the [[Rugby Football League Championship|Championship]] and the [[Super League]]. He also played for [[Sale Sharks]] in the [[English Premiership (rugby union)|Zurich Championship]] and [[North Harbour Rugby Union|North Harbour]] in New Zealand. Perelini played both {{rlp|pr}} and {{rlp|sr}} in league, and as a back row player in union.

In the [[1991 Rugby Union World Cup]] he played as an open side flanker, in the Samoans' four games (against Scotland, Wales, Argentina and Australia). During the World Cup he gained the nickname of 'Terminator' for his fierce, hard-hitting tackling technique. In 1994 he received an approach from [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] to play rugby league in England. At first he rejected it, but finally agreed. In 1995 he played for rugby league for [[Samoa national rugby league team|Samoa]] in the Rugby League World Cup, making him the first Samoan to play in World Cups in both codes.

Perelini played for St Helens at {{rlp|pr}} in the [[1996 Challenge Cup]] Final, scoring a try in the last minutes and helping his team to a 40–32 victory over the [[Bradford Bulls]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Steve Prescott Foundation|url=http://www.steveprescottfoundation.co.uk/About/StevePrescottStats.aspx|publisher=Steve Prescott Foundation|access-date=3 June 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120808052156/http://www.steveprescottfoundation.co.uk/About/StevePrescottStats.aspx|archive-date=8 August 2012|url-status=dead}}</ref> At the end of [[Super League]]'s first season, Perelini was named at {{rlp|pr}} in the 1996 [[Super League Dream Team]].

Apollo Perelini played at {{rlp|pr}} in [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]' 16–25 defeat by [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[1995–96 League Cup (rugby league)|1995–96 Regal Trophy]] Final during the [[1995–96 Rugby Football League season|1995–96]] at [[John Smith's Stadium|Alfred McAlpine Stadium]], [[Huddersfield]] on Saturday 13 January 1996.<ref name="13th January 1996: St Helens 16 Wigan 25 (Regal Trophy Final)">{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2976|title=13th January 1996: St Helens 16 Wigan 25 (Regal Trophy Final)|publisher=Wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181007005311/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2976|archive-date=7 October 2018|url-status=dead}}</ref>

Perelini played for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] at {{rlp|pr}} in their [[1999 Super League Grand Final]] victory over [[Bradford Bulls]], having won this they contested in the [[2000 World Club Challenge]] against [[National Rugby League]] Premiers the [[Melbourne Storm]], with Perelini playing from the interchange bench in the loss. He also played for St. Helens at {{rlp|pr}} in their [[2000 Super League Grand Final]] victory over [[Wigan Warriors]].

At the age of 33, after finishing his rugby league playing career, he returned to rugby union to play for the [[Sale Sharks]].

==Post playing==
After retiring from playing he joined the Sale's Coaching staff. In 2004 he returned to St Helens as the team's Head of Strength and Conditioning and Skills coach. Apollo helped St Helens to four Challenge Cup titles (2004, 2006, 2007 and 2008), four League Leaders titles (2005, 2006, 2007 and 2008), one World Club Championship (2007) and one Grand Final Winners (2006, the year in which St Helens won the BBC sports Team Of The Year) <ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/player/41|title=Saints » Player|access-date=21 June 2007|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070402212416/http://www.saintsrlfc.com/player/41|archive-date=2 April 2007}}</ref>

He now lives in [[Dubai]] where he was formerly the Director of Sport at [[Repton School Dubai]], a [[private school]]. He currently runs two sporting Academies - Apollo Perelini Rugby Skills Academy (www.aprsa.com) and JETS (a female football academy), at an increasing number of venues around the emirate.

After nearly a decade in charge of the [[UAE national rugby team|UAE National Rugby team]],<ref>{{Cite news |title=UAE International Rugby Team Squad Profiles 2017 |url=https://yallasports.ae/rugby/uae-rugby/team/ |access-date=2018-07-27 |work=Yalla Rugby |language=en-GB}}</ref> a position which he started in 2015 for the UAERF, Apollo [https://yallasports.ae/news/rugby/apollo-perelini-steps-down-after-transforming-uae-rugby/ announced is retirement] from the role on 24 November 2025. Apollo spoke about what makes the Rugby Sevens so special and the role that rugby has in the [[Dubai]] history in the [[This Luxury Life]] podcast show. 
<ref>{{cite web|url=https://podcasts.apple.com/ae/podcast/episode-17-the-rugby-sevens/id1449632900?i=1000458987085|title=Episode 17: The Rugby Sevens|access-date=2020-02-10}}</ref>
